skill.name: molfeat
skill.description: Guidance for using molfeat to convert SMILES and RDKit molecules into machine learning features for cheminformatics workflows.
skill.summary: Use molfeat featurizers, descriptors, and pretrained embeddings for molecular machine learning.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found they are mostly false positives from markdown code formatting and chemistry terminology. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, or malicious execution path was found; the main publishable warning is example code that loads pickle cache files.

medium_findings.title: Unsafe Pickle Cache Pattern in Examples
medium_findings.description: The documentation demonstrates loading embeddings from a local pickle cache. This is not malicious, but copied code could execute arbitrary code if the cache file is attacker controlled.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is semantically real because pickle.load reads a local file. Confidence is below very high because it is documentation example code, not code executed by the skill itself.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Formatting Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Most external command findings are inline code or fenced examples in markdown. They describe molfeat APIs or installation commands and are not executed by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are markdown bullets or fenced code blocks, not executable Ruby backticks. The surrounding context is installation and API documentation.

low_findings.title: Chemistry Terms Misclassified as Malware Indicators
low_findings.description: Weak crypto and C2 keyword alerts match chemistry and machine learning names such as MACCS, ECFP, desc2D, CATS, and model identifiers.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious tokens are documented featurizer names and descriptor categories. No command-and-control behavior, cryptographic operation, or malicious endpoint is present in this context.

low_findings.title: Reference URLs Are Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to molfeat documentation, GitHub, PyPI, and a tutorial. I found no evidence of data exfiltration or tracking code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are listed as user-facing resources in a references section. There is no code that sends local data or secrets to these destinations.

low_findings.title: Examples Encourage Full CPU Parallelism
low_findings.description: Several examples use n_jobs=-1 to consume all CPU cores. This is a legitimate molfeat pattern, but users should avoid it on shared systems.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is clearly documented and legitimate for local ML workloads. The residual risk is operational resource exhaustion rather than malicious behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unsafe Pickle Cache Pattern in Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation demonstrates loading embeddings from a local pickle cache. This is not malicious, but copied code could execute arbitrary code if the cache file is attacker controlled.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is semantically real because pickle.load reads a local file. Confidence is below very high because it is documentation example code, not code executed by the skill itself.

content.value_statement: Molecular ML work needs reliable numeric features from chemical structures. This skill guides molfeat setup, featurizer selection, and workflow design.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use calculators, transformers, and pretrained molfeat transformers.
Compares fingerprints, descriptors, graph models, and transformer embeddings for molecular ML.
Guides SMILES and RDKit molecule featurization for batch workflows.
Provides examples for QSAR modeling, similarity search, and virtual screening.
Documents serialization, caching, error handling, and dependency choices.
limitations: Does not install molfeat or run molecular computations by itself.
Requires valid chemistry inputs and compatible optional dependencies for advanced models.
Pretrained models can be slow and may require significant memory.
Generated guidance still needs domain validation before scientific decisions.

use_cases.title: Build QSAR Features
use_cases.description: Select fingerprints or descriptors and prepare feature matrices for property prediction models.

use_cases.title: Screen Compound Libraries
use_cases.description: Choose fast featurizers for similarity search, clustering, and virtual screening workflows.

use_cases.title: Prototype Molecular ML Pipelines
use_cases.description: Combine molfeat transformers with scikit-learn or PyTorch workflows for experiments.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Featurizer
prompt_templates.prompt: Recommend a molfeat featurizer for my task: target property, dataset size, model type, and need for interpretability.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Batch Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a molfeat workflow for a SMILES dataset with invalid molecules, batching, caching, and reproducible configuration.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Feature Families
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare ECFP, MACCS, RDKit descriptors, Mordred, and ChemBERTa for this molecular ML problem.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Pipeline Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my molfeat pipeline for leakage, featurizer fit, resource use, serialization risks, and evaluation design.

output_examples.input: I need interpretable features for a small solubility dataset.
output_examples.output: The skill would recommend RDKit 2D descriptors, scaling, cross-validation, and documentation of descriptor names for interpretation.

output_examples.input: I want fast similarity search over a large compound library.
output_examples.output: The skill would suggest ECFP or MAP4, batching, controlled parallelism, and validation of invalid SMILES handling.

output_examples.input: I need pretrained molecular embeddings for transfer learning.
output_examples.output: The skill would compare ChemBERTa, ChemGPT, and GNN options, then note dependency, memory, and caching requirements.

best_practices: Start with simple fingerprints or descriptors before using pretrained models.
Save featurizer configuration and package versions for reproducible experiments.
Use controlled parallelism and validate invalid SMILES handling on a sample first.
anti_patterns: Do not choose a featurizer only because it has the largest dimension.
Do not load pickle caches from untrusted or shared locations.
Do not compare models without the same train, validation, and test splits.

faq.question: What is molfeat used for?
faq.answer: Molfeat converts molecules into numerical features for machine learning, similarity search, QSAR, and virtual screening.

faq.question: Can it work with SMILES strings?
faq.answer: Yes. The documentation shows calculators and transformers that accept SMILES strings and RDKit molecule objects.

faq.question: Which featurizer should I try first?
faq.answer: For many baseline models, start with ECFP or RDKit 2D descriptors before testing larger pretrained embeddings.

faq.question: Does this skill run computations automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. Users must install dependencies and run code in their own environment.

faq.question: Are pretrained models included?
faq.answer: The skill documents ChemBERTa, ChemGPT, GIN, Graphormer, and other models available through molfeat workflows.

faq.question: What security caution matters most?
faq.answer: Avoid loading pickle cache files from untrusted sources because pickle can execute code during loading.
